codeReview.summary: Solid implementation that covers all core requirements. Added ToolType.wifiHeatmap to shared Enums.swift, ToolDestination.wifiHeatmap to ToolsView.swift, a ToolItem entry in the tools grid, HeatmapDashboardViewModel with filesystem-based project loading/sorting/deletion, HeatmapDashboardView with project list, empty state, loading state, and new project button using liquid glass styling, plus 9 well-structured ViewModel unit tests. Both iOS and macOS builds pass. All issues found are non-blocking.

issues.description: ToolType.wifiHeatmap uses .cyan color, which is already used by .vpnInfo (line 250). Two tools in the grid share the same accent color, making them less visually distinct.

Some accessibility identifiers use 2-part naming (e.g., 'heatmap_emptyState', 'heatmap_loadingState', 'heatmap_projectList') instead of the documented 3-part convention '{screen}_{element}_{descriptor}'. Should be e.g. 'heatmap_section_emptyState', 'heatmap_section_loadingState', 'heatmap_section_projectList'.

HeatmapProjectSummary.formattedDate creates a new DateFormatter on every access. For a list of projects this means N formatter allocations per render cycle. A static formatter would be more efficient, though for typical project counts (<50) the impact is negligible.

The 'New' button and 'Create New Project' button both set showNewProjectSheet = true, but the original commit has no .sheet modifier to present anything. This is acceptable since NewProjectView is delivered by a subsequent feature (ios-heatmap-floor-plan-import), but means at commit time clicking these buttons has no visible effect.

loadProjects() performs synchronous filesystem I/O (directory listing + JSON decoding) on the MainActor. For large numbers of projects or bundles with complex survey.json, this could cause UI jank. An async version with Task would be more robust, though for typical project counts this is acceptable.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker used 2-part accessibility identifiers (e.g., 'heatmap_emptyState', 'heatmap_projectList') alongside correct 3-part ones (e.g., 'heatmap_screen_dashboard', 'heatmap_button_newProject'). The mission AGENTS.md documents the 3-part pattern but doesn't enforce it or provide guidance on when a 2-part form is acceptable. This leads to inconsistency across features.

Worker did not follow TDD (Step 2 of ios-ui-worker skill: 'Write ViewModel/Logic Tests First — Red Phase'). Implementation was done first, tests written after. Handoff says followedProcedure: true but suggestedChanges acknowledges skipping TDD for filesystem-dependent ViewModels. The skill's TDD requirement may be impractical for integration-style ViewModels that need the implementation to define the test surface.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Transcript skeleton shows: Edit (Enums.swift) → Edit (ToolsView.swift) → Create (HeatmapDashboardViewModel.swift) → Create (HeatmapDashboardView.swift) → Create (HeatmapDashboardViewModelTests.swift). Tests came last, not first. Handoff skillFeedback.suggestedChanges: 'Consider relaxing the strict TDD requirement for integration-style ViewModels.'

sharedStateObservations.observation: No convention exists about unique colors for ToolType entries. Worker reused .cyan for wifiHeatmap which is already used by vpnInfo. Without guidance, future tools may also duplicate colors, reducing visual distinction in the tools grid.

summary: Feature passes review. The implementation correctly adds the Wi-Fi Heatmap tool to the iOS Tools tab with proper navigation, creates a well-structured dashboard with project list, empty state, and new project button, uses liquid glass styling throughout, and includes 9 comprehensive ViewModel tests. All 6 expected behaviors from the feature spec are satisfied (with the new project creation flow dependent on the next feature delivering NewProjectView). Five non-blocking issues identified: duplicate tool color, inconsistent accessibility ID naming, DateFormatter allocation pattern, no-op new project button at commit time, and synchronous MainActor I/O. Three shared state observations recorded about accessibility ID conventions, TDD skill guidance, and tool color uniqueness.
